cot_prefix: Okay, let's see. The question is about a 1974 World Bank study mentioned in the passage. The task is to determine what the study found regarding the benefits of economic growth in some developing countries. First, I need to recall the relevant part of the text. Let me go through the passage again. The key part is in the third paragraph: "A major study by the World Bank in 1974 concluded that it is now clear that more than decades of rapid growth in developing countries has been of little benefit to a third of their population." So the study's conclusion was that despite decades of rapid growth, only a third of the population benefited. That means the majority (two thirds) didn't see much benefit. The options given are: (A) benefit to a third of their population (B) benefit to two thirds of their population (C) little benefit to their people (D) no benefit at all to their people Looking at the wording of the study's conclusion: "little benefit to a third of their population." Wait, that seems counterintuitive. If a third had little benefit, then two thirds might have had more. But the wording here is tricky. Let me parse it again. The sentence says that the growth was "of little benefit to a third of their population." That might mean that a third had little benefit, but the rest (the remaining two thirds) perhaps had more. However, the way the sentence is structured, the phrase "little benefit to a third of their population" might mean that for the entire population, the benefit was limited to a third. Or it could mean that a third of the population didn't benefit. But the way it's written, "more than decades of rapid growth in developing countries has been of little benefit to a third of their population." So the growth didn't help a third of the population much. But what about the rest? The implication is that the majority (the other two-thirds) did benefit from the growth? But the passage later says, "increase in GNP per capita did not promise important improvements in such development indicators as nutrition, health, and education. Although GNP per capita did indeed rise, its benefit came down to only a small part of the population."
